title: Mow this lawn. Learn about your brain.
description: Your lawn mowing style says more about you than you think.

content.value: This is a story about how our minds work. But first, let’s start with a game: how efficiently can you mow this lawn?
So far, X other people have mowed this lawn. Your technique was better than X% of them.
If we strip away the grass, mowing is really just a path of connected nodes. You were X% less efficient than the optimal path, which looks like this:The optimal path looks like this (TODO):

Finding the best path through the nodes is a classic computer science problem. No, don’t leave! I promise this won’t get too wonky. All you need to know is that there are two main ways to tackle a problem like this:

li: algorithms which calculate the optimal path and
heuristics which are simple strategies to complete the path

content.value: Humans tend to operate like heuristics, and based on your path, you mow most like a __.
<mark>Reader’s path vs their matching heuristic</mark>

This isn’t a perfect label though, we used some fancy calculations to find the closest match. You can read more about it in the method section. Your detailed heuristic classification breakdown looks like this:
<mark>small bar chart of similarity scores across all heuristics</mark>
There are many strategies for a problem like mowing the lawn, which is technically known as Coverage Path Planning. Here are some common techniques:
<mark>small multiple grid of all heuristics</mark>
On an open grid with few obstacles, most of these techniques (and those performed by other readers) performed almost identically.
<mark>bar chart/table of their efficiency scores and how many users fell into each.</mark>
The differences only start to become apparent when things get more complicated. On a small, simple grid your approach doesn’t matter much. But what happens when the grid gets bigger?
Is mowing not your thing? Pick something more familiar to prime your mind.
<mark>reader plays a larger grid, 14x14</mark>
<mark>[their new path animated and its scoring</mark>
On the small grid you were a __. On the big grid you shifted to a __.
Or
You stuck with the __ technique, even as complexity grew. That’s [common/common] like % of others.
Why does this matter? When different obstacles appear, some strategies hold up better than others.
<mark>slope chart of small vs. large for each heuristic</mark>
Here is how everyone performed across both grids.
<mark>[slope chart again with internet and you</mark>
This is why heuristics exist. When problems get complex enough that finding the perfect answer is impractical, we fall back on good-enough strategies. So do computers.
The Coverage Path Planning is related to one of the most famous problems in computer science: The Traveling Salesman Problem.

value.figcaption: From The Coding Train with Daniel Shiffman

content.value: The premise is simple: a salesman must visit a set of cities exactly once and return to their starting point, all while taking the shortest possible route. We see this all the time in the real world. From a pizza delivery driver planning their stops to a trick-or-treater maximizing their candy haul, finding the most efficient routes would be solving a version of this problem.

I wanted to create this mowing experiment after I read a study that found humans come impressively close to computer-calculated optimal solutions to this problem with fewer stops, and even do pretty good — just 11% less efficient than optimal — for 70+ stops. But as the stops increase, humans’ ability decreases.

value.alt: A chart depicting how people perform on the Traveling Salesman Problem compared to the optimal path, which shows that people perform near-ideal at fewer nodes, with a slow increasing deviation as more nodes are added
value.figcaption: Chart of findings from this study
